In the context of materials science, a catalyst is a chemical agent that increases the rate of a reaction by lowering the activation energy required for the reaction to occur. This means that a catalyst can speed up a reaction without being consumed in the process.

Catalytic concentration refers to the measure of catalytic activity per unit volume in materials science. Electric charge is the quality of having an imbalance of electric charge, which is a fundamental conserved property of some subatomic particles. Electric flux density, in the context of materials science, refers to the measure of the amount of electric charge over a given plane. Electrophilic addition is a type of polar addition reaction in materials science where a pi bond is removed by the creation of two new covalent bonds. In this reaction, an electrophile (electron-deficient species) attacks a pi bond in a molecule, leading to the formation of two new covalent bonds. This process is known as electrophilic addition because the electrophile is attracted to the electron-rich pi bond, resulting in the addition of the electrophile to the molecule.

Electrostatic interaction refers to the attraction or repulsion between charged particles, such as electrons, that are not shared between atoms or molecules. In materials science, this type of interaction plays a significant role in determining the properties of materials, such as their conductivity, dielectric properties, and stability.

In materials science, an element is a pure substance that cannot be decomposed or transformed into other chemical substances by ordinary chemical processes. It is made up of atoms with the same atomic number.

PermittivityUnit refers to the standard unit used to measure permittivity, which is a material property that describes how much electric field can be stored in a material. Zirconium is also commonly used as an alloying agent in materials to improve their mechanical properties.
